texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Rubbery polymeric membranes, such as polydimethyl siloxane (PDMS) are well known to be highly permeable, but have a poor ability to sieve molecules based on size [1]. Overall selectivity is thus generally governed by differences in penetrant solubility [2,3]. These membranes have found use in applications where it is desirable to retain a small molecule but allow larger, more condensable gases such as hydrocarbons to permeate. For example, PDMShasbeenconsideredfortheseparationofhydrocarbonsfrom natural gas [4,5] and for the separation of both hydrocarbons and perfluorocarbons from permanent gases [6], along with hydrocarbon separation from methane and hydrogen, under mixed gas conditions [7]. This interest has meant that the polymer permeability, solubility, diffusivity and dilation upon exposure to a number of penetrants are well characterized under both pure [2,8,9] and mixedgas[4-6]conditions. In particular, a number of workers have shownthat swelling or dilation of the polymer matrix by hydrocarbon and perflurocarbon vapors leads to more rapid diffusion of all gases.

This interest has meant that the polymer permeability, solubility, diffusivity and dilation upon exposure to a number of penetrants are well characterized under both pure [2,8,9] and mixedgas[4-6]conditions. texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Rubbery polymeric membranes demonstrate potential in gas separation as they can be selective for carbon dioxide over less condensable gases such as hydrogen. In many industrial processes where these membranesare applicable, minor gas components such as carbon monoxide, hydrogen sulfide and water are present. Here, the effect of these minor components on the performance of a polydimethyl siloxane rubbery membrane in CO2/N2 separation is experimentally determined. The permeability of CO2 through PDMS is reduced upon exposure to CO, H2S and water, due to competitive sorption of these gases into the polymeric matrix. Similar behavior is observed for N2; however the presence of H2S gives rise to an observed increase in N2 permeability. Extension of the Flory-Huggins theory to a quaternary system allows the relevant interaction parameters for this system to be determined. Upon exposure to a wet feed, CO2 and N2 permeability decrease due to water occupying free volume within PDMS, with the Flory-Huggins interaction parameters indicating mixing of CO2 or N2 with water within PDMS is highly unfavorable. Quantitative analysis of the change in permeability of CO2 and N2 in PDMS in the presence of these minor components enables more accurate prediction of membrane performance in industrial applications to be undertaken.

The permeability of CO2 through PDMS is reduced upon exposure to CO, H2S and water, due to competitive sorption of these gases into the polymeric matrix. Similar behavior is observed for N2; however the presence of H2S gives rise to an observed increase in N2 permeability. Extension of the Flory-Huggins theory to a quaternary system allows the relevant interaction parameters for this system to be determined. Upon exposure to a wet feed, CO2 and N2 permeability decrease due to water occupying free volume within PDMS, with the Flory-Huggins interaction parameters indicating mixing of CO2 or N2 with water within PDMS is highly unfavorable. Quantitative analysis of the change in permeability of CO2 and N2 in PDMS in the presence of these minor components enables more accurate prediction of membrane performance in industrial applications to be undertaken.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The potential use of PDMS for hydrogen gas separation processes, such as syngas treatment, is also of interest [10,11]. In this application it is desirable to keep the small hydrogen molecule at high pressure and allow the larger more condensable gases to permeate through the membrane. In syngas processing, CO2 permeation through such a membrane allows pre-combustion carbon capture, while ensuring hydrogen remains at sufficient pressure for combustion as part of an Integrated Gasification and Combined Cycle (IGCC) process [12]. If air-blown IGCC is employed, such as in IDGCC (Integrated Drying Gasification and Combined Cycle) then N2 is also present in large quantities and is also preferred to be kept at high pressures [12].
texts.text: The potential use of PDMS for hydrogen gas separation processes, such as syngas treatment, is also of interest [10,11]. In this application it is desirable to keep the small hydrogen molecule at high pressure and allow the larger more condensable gases to permeate through the membrane. In syngas processing, CO2 permeation through such a membrane allows pre-combustion carbon capture, while ensuring hydrogen remains at sufficient pressure for combustion as part of an Integrated Gasification and Combined Cycle (IGCC) process [12]. If air-blown IGCC is employed, such as in IDGCC (Integrated Drying Gasification and Combined Cycle) then N2 is also present in large quantities and is also preferred to be kept at high pressures [12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, in such a syngas purification process, many other gas species are present, such as H2 O at near saturated conditions, along with H2S, CO and NH3 in trace amounts [13]. The presence of these components will subsequently influence the CO2 separation from both H2 and N2, as they compete for sorption within the polymeric matrix. Little is known about the influence of such contaminants on the behavior of rubbery polymers such as PDMS. In this work, the permeability of a CO2/N2 mixture through PDMS is studied upon exposure to CO, H2S and H2O individually at different partial pressures. This competition for sorption within the polymeric matrix is then quantified in terms of the Flory-Huggins theory of mixing in polymers. The results will enable prediction of PDMS membrane performance in industrial systems.

texts.text: 4. Experimental

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polydimethyl siloxane composite membranes (PERVAP 4060) were supplied by Sulzer Chemtech (Switzerland). The composite membranes were composed of a PDMS film on a highly microporous support, with PDMS thickness ∼ 10 𝛍 m. For sorption studies, densePDMSblockswereproducedfromSylgard184(DowCorning, Michigan), with cross-linking occurring at ambient temperature using a ratio of 10 parts base to 1 part crosslinker/catalyst. After 30min, the viscous solution was cast into films and allowed to cure for 2 days to produce films of thickness ∼ 105 𝛍 m. Pure gases of N2 (high purity), CO2 (industrial grade), helium (ultrahigh purity), a 90% N2/10% CO2 mixture, as well as 90% N2/10% CO2 mixtures with 1000ppm CO or 500ppm H2S were supplied by BOC Gas Ltd.

texts.orig: Single gas permeabilities were measured on a constant volume, variable pressure gas permeation apparatus described elsewhere [22]. Mixed gas permeabilities of PDMS membranes were tested on a mixed gas instrument, also reported elsewhere [23]. The PDMS films were mounted on a porous stainless steel support in a 47 mm test cell, which was placed in an oven maintained at 15, 35, 55 or 75 ◦ C. A feed stream of 90% N2/10% CO2 gas mixture was passed across the retentate side of the membrane, while helium sweep gas passed across the permeate side to prevent concentration polarization. The retentate pressure was varied while the permeate side was at atmospheric pressure. Flow rates were maintained and monitored by digital mass flow controllers (Aalborg), with a back pressure regulator on the retentate (Extech Technologies). Each membrane was exposed for 1h until steady-state CO2/N2 separation has been obtained. The feed gas was then changed to 90% N2/10%CO2 with500ppmH2Sgasmixtureor90%N2/10%CO2 with 1000ppm CO, with pressure and temperature conditions the same as before. The permeability of CO2 and N2 was measured every 15minuntilsteady-state conditions were achieved. Error was evaluated as twice the standard deviation of three measurements at each temperature and pressure.

texts.orig: The concentration isotherms of CO2, N2 and water within PDMS at different temperatures are shown in Figs. 2-4 respectively. The isotherms of all three gases are essentially linear, which is consistent with the literature reported behavior for small gases in rubbery polymers [25]. Both the CO2 and N2 isotherms, at 35 ◦ C, are in good agreement with the literature [25,26]. The consistency of our results with others (Fig. 5) confirms that the solubility parameter is independent of membrane manufacturing methods or the extent of cross-linking.

texts.orig: The water concentration isotherms highlight that water is considerably less soluble in PDMS than CO2 even though it is a more condensable gas. This is attributed to the hydrophobic nature of PDMS and hence water sorption into the PDMS matrix is unfavorable. The isotherm is somewhat convex at high pressures with respect to the pressure axis, which is typical for highly condensable vapors in PDMS [27]. In this situation, the pressure being near saturation leads to clustering of water molecules within the polymer [28], given the hydrophobic polymeric environment and the strong hydrogen-bondingpotentialofwater.Thisregionofthecurveisnot included in determining the Flory-Huggins interaction parameter, provided in Table 2, since it requires more complex models [15] than are justified by the purpose of this work.

texts.orig: The Flory-Huggins interaction parameters for CO and H2S in PDMS were not determined experimentally. To estimate these parameters, published values for other gases and liquid in PDMS at 20-35 ◦ C were plotted against a variety of parameters, including the critical temperature, critical pressure, boiling point and dipole moment. A clear correlation was obtained only with the kinetic diameter of the gases (Fig. 6). Kamiya et al. [30] demonstrate a similar relationship between /US and molar volume for large molecules, however for the smaller species under consideration in this work, the correlation between /US and molar volume was less clear. Therefore, based on the kinetic diameter of CO being 3.76Å and H2S being 3.6 Å [13], the Flory-Huggins parameters are estimated and provided in Table 2.

(17) (Table 6), assuming all parameters from the binary and tertiary systems are constant. The Henry's law constant for CO in PDMS was taken from the work of Kamiya et al. [30]. CO2-CO and N2-CO Flory-Huggins parameters are of similar value to each other and are greater than either gas's interaction parameter with PDMS. This implies that in this quaternary system it is more favorable for all three gases to exist separately within regions of the PDMS membrane than mix together.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeability of CO2 and N2 through PDMS upon exposure to different partial pressures of water is shown in Figs. 13 and 14 respectively. Upon exposure to the wet feed, the permeability of both CO2 and N2 is reduced, due to competition from water, with a trend of decreasing permeability with increased humidity of the feed. For this quaternary system, CO2-N2-H2O-PDMS, the Flory-Huggins interaction parameters between CO2-H2O and N2-H2O within PDMS can be determined from Eq. (17) and are provided in Table 6, again assuming all parameters from the binary and tertiary systems are constant. The Flory-Huggins interaction parameter between water and either CO2 or N2 is of the greatest magnitude of any of the interaction parameters determined here, double that of water association with PDMS. Hence, the mixing of either of these two gases with water within PDMS is extremely unlikely, and it is energetically more favorable for water to be alone within regions of the PDMS matrix, at the exclusion of CO2 and N2. This therefore restricts considerable volume within PDMS and results in the observed reductions in permeability upon exposure to wet feeds. Based on Flory-Huggins mixing theory, the determined interaction parameters for the mixing of these gases within PDMS,allowsthechangeinpermeabilityofbothCO2 andN2 within the membrane upon exposure to a feed consisting of a mixture of these gases to be quantitatively associated with the influence of each component.

texts.orig: 6. Conclusion
texts.text: 6. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeability of CO2 and N2 through a PDMS membrane is reduced from a single gas case to a mixed gas scenario, due to com- petitive sorption within the polymeric matrix. Further reduction in both CO2 and N2 permeabilities is observed when the system is exposed separately to both CO and water at the ppm level. Conversely, N2 permeability increases slightly in the presence of H2S. By extending the Flory-Huggins theory to a quaternary system, the Flory-HugginsinteractionparametersforCO2 andN2 withinPDMS, along with the interactions between themselves and CO, H2S as well as water have been determined. These interaction parameters allowed quantitative conclusions to be made about the mixing of gases within PDMS.

Conversely, N2 permeability increases slightly in the presence of H2S. By extending the Flory-Huggins theory to a quaternary system, the Flory-HugginsinteractionparametersforCO2 andN2 withinPDMS, along with the interactions between themselves and CO, H2S as well as water have been determined. These interaction parameters allowed quantitative conclusions to be made about the mixing of gases within PDMS.
